pricing: We charge a monthly subscription per active employee, roughly $2‑$5 per head depending on the feature tier, with a small setup fee for onboarding.
traction: We have a handful of beta users who are currently testing the platform, but we haven't closed any paying contracts yet. Early feedback is positive about the UI, but we’re still working on proving real ROI.
team: I'm Alex Rivera, former HR analyst at a national retailer and the sole founder. I’ve also built a small internal compliance tool in my previous role that sparked this idea.
problem: Multi‑state employers struggle to keep up with the constantly shifting mosaic of state‑specific labor laws, which leads to missed filings, fines, and a lot of manual work.
solution: ComplianceBridge aggregates state regulations into a single view, sends automated reminders for key deadlines, and provides template documents that can be customized for each jurisdiction.

name: ClaSync
description: ClaSync is a lightweight CLM platform built for procurement teams that need to keep contracts in sync across multiple suppliers. We combine AI‑powered clause extraction with a simple approval workflow so spend analysts can focus on negotiation rather than paperwork.
target_customer: Procurement departments in mid‑size enterprises (≈$50‑500M annual spend) – the procurement lead pays because it cuts legal review time and reduces maverick spend.
pricing: Subscription per active contract, tiered: Starter $1,200/mo up to 150 contracts, Growth $2,800/mo up to 500 contracts, Enterprise custom pricing.
traction: Beta launched 6 months ago with 3 pilot customers; average time‑to‑signature fell 22%. We have two letters of intent in the pipeline and a small but engaged user group, but references are still limited.
team: I'm Alex Rivera, former senior analyst at a Fortune 200 procurement org who built and sold a SaaS tool. My co‑founder Maya Patel left a Big‑Four consulting firm; currently we're a two‑person team with a part‑time developer.
problem: Procurement teams juggle dozens of supplier contracts stored in email, SharePoint, or spreadsheets, leading to missed renewals, non‑compliant clauses, and hidden spend.
solution: ClaSync centralises contracts, auto‑extracts key terms with a transformer model, flags renewal dates, and pushes approvals through Slack/Teams, giving a single source of truth and reducing manual effort.

name: ContinuumShield
description: ContinuumShield is a cloud‑based platform that helps small‑ and medium‑size businesses build, test, and automate their business continuity plans. We combine a simple drag‑and‑drop workflow builder with real‑time impact simulations so owners can see exactly how a disruption would affect revenue, staff, and customers. The goal is to turn continuity planning from a yearly checklist into an ongoing, data‑driven process.
target_customer: The primary buyer is the operations manager or the office manager of an SMB (50‑200 employees) who is responsible for risk mitigation, but the ultimate payer is the CFO or CEO who needs to justify the cost of downtime avoidance.
pricing: We’re currently piloting a subscription model with tiers that range roughly from $49 / month for a “starter” plan (basic workflows, 1 simulation per month) up to $299 / month for an “enterprise” plan (unlimited simulations, API access, multi‑site support). We haven’t locked in the final structure yet and are still testing usage‑based add‑ons, so the exact pricing roadmap is still a work in progress.
traction: In the last 6 months we’ve signed 12 paying beta customers and logged over 300 simulated disruptions, with churn under 5 %. Users report a 30‑40 % reduction in time spent on continuity planning and have begun to reference our dashboards in board meetings, indicating early product‑market fit.
team: I’m Alex Rivera, former senior product manager at a disaster‑recovery SaaS that was acquired by a Fortune‑500 firm; my co‑founder Maya Patel led risk‑management consulting for mid‑market firms before moving into product development.
problem: SMBs often treat business continuity as a one‑off, paper‑based exercise that quickly becomes outdated, leaving them vulnerable to IT outages, supply‑chain shocks, or natural disasters with no clear response plan.
solution: ContinuumShield digitizes the entire continuity lifecycle: users map critical processes, assign owners, run automated impact simulations, and receive actionable recovery scripts that are kept current in real time. The platform also sends alerts and assigns tasks when a real incident is detected, ensuring plans are executed without delay.

name: PermitPulse
description: PermitPulse is a cloud‑based platform that lets small municipalities digitize, track, and approve building and zoning permits in a single workflow. We hook into the city’s existing GIS and accounting systems so staff can see a resident’s application status in real time.
target_customer: City clerks and planning department directors in towns under 50,000 residents – they buy because it trims the manual paperwork backlog and reduces citizen complaints about “lost” permits.
pricing: Subscription per municipality: $250 / month for the core workflow, $400 / month for the full integration (GIS, payment gateway, and custom reporting). Discounts for multi‑year contracts.
traction: Beta launched with three towns (population 8‑32 K) that collectively processed 1,200 permits in the first 8 weeks, cutting average approval time from 14 days to 4. Two of them have signed 12‑month contracts; one is still evaluating.
team: I’m Alex Rivera, former GIS analyst for a county government and a product lead at a mid‑size civic‑tech SaaS. My co‑founder Maya Patel spent five years building workflow automation tools at a fintech startup.
problem: Small towns still rely on paper forms, email chains, and spreadsheets to manage permits, leading to lost documents, delayed approvals, and frustrated residents.
solution: PermitPulse provides a simple web portal where applicants upload files, pay fees, and see status updates; staff route applications through customizable stages, auto‑validate code compliance, and generate certificates—all without requiring a full IT department.
